learning_outcomes.description: Explain the growth and development of special interest tourism over the past 30 years.
Analyse and compare the characteristics of a range of different types of tourism niche and micro-niche markets.
Evaluate market evidence from tourist, operator and destination perspectives to judge what matters for a named client and context.
Direct AI tools to gather and structure market evidence, then interrogate the output for sources, gaps and unfounded claims.
Design and justify a special interest tour for a named real-world client and community, working independently or in a team.

assessments.task_description:
This in-person exam assesses students’ foundational understanding of special interest tourism concepts introduced in the first four weeks of the course. Students will demonstrate their knowledge of key theories, tourist motivations, market contexts, destination considerations, and operator issues relevant to special interest tourism.

The exam may include knowledge-based and applied questions requiring students to define concepts, interpret examples, and apply course material to special interest tourism contexts. It is designed to test individual understanding and prepare students for the later market analysis and special interest tour design assessments.

This is a secure, in-person assessment. Students must attend at the scheduled time and will be required to verify their identity. Artificial intelligence tools, translation tools, communication tools, and unauthorised materials are not permitted unless explicitly approved by the Course Coordinator.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.assessment_title: Special Interest Tourism Market Analysis (incl. tour reflection)
assessments.task_description:
A two-part assessment in which a low-stakes tour reflection contributes to a broader market analysis for The Brisbane Greeters.

This assessment requires students to attend an in-person guided tour and analyse the market context for selected special interest tourism types. Students will use this evidence to identify opportunities, constraints, and implications for tour design. The task is completed in two connected parts.

In Part A, students individually complete a short tour reflection based on their experience of participating in a guided tour during the current semester. The reflection requires students to critically consider the tourist experience, target market, tour theme, key moments, interpretation, and overall experience.

Students are strongly encouraged to attend a Brisbane Greeters Walking Tour. These tours are free and take place daily at 10:00 am. Students can sign up here: https://www.brisbane.qld.gov.au/whats-on-in-brisbane/brisbane-greeter-tours

Students may attend a different guided special interest tour; however, they must seek approval from the teaching team before completing the assessment to confirm that the tour is suitable.

In Part B, students complete a market analysis for two types of special interest tourism markets relevant to The Brisbane Greeters. The analysis should draw on appropriate evidence to examine tourist demand, market characteristics, competitor or comparable experiences, destination opportunities, operational considerations, and relevant social, cultural, environmental, or commercial factors. Students are expected to use this analysis to identify what matters for the named client and context.

The use of AI tools is an expected part of Part B. Students record how they directed AI tools to gather and structure market evidence, and how they checked the output for sources, gaps and unfounded claims, in a Decision and Evidence Log submitted as an appendix within the report.

Together, Part A and Part B provide the evidence base for the later Special Interest Tour Design task.

Students submit the report as a single document via Turnitin on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission via Blackboard, subject to originality, and plagiarism review.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
30/10/2026 5:00 pm

Submission to Blackboard, if working in a team, only one team member needs to make a submission.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment requires students to design a special interest micro-niche tour for The Brisbane Greeters and pitch it as a recorded video (5 minute duration maximum). The design must build directly on the A2 market analysis and justify key decisions using evidence about the target market, destination context, operator needs, and named client/community.

Students may complete the task individually or in a team of up to three. The tour must be set in Brisbane, designed for a small group, include a clear special interest theme, and feature structured storytelling and one memorable WOW moment.

Students submit:

  1. Recorded video pitch,
  2. The completed tour design template,
  3. A supporting promotional brochure. 

This is an AI-open task, but students must critically check AI output and ensure the final design and justification reflect their own reasoning.

This assessment can be completed individually or in groups of 3 students (maximum). 

Further details and guidance will be provided on Blackboard and in class on selection of tour themes, locations, length of tour, etc.

The template of design elements will be provided on Blackboard and explained in class.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Interactive Oral Exam (individual defence of the design)
assessments.due_date:
End of Semester Exam Period

7/11/2026 - 21/11/2026

Exam block week 2.
assessments.task_description:
This live, in-person oral exam requires students to defend the reasoning behind their special interest tour design. Students respond to questions about their rationale behind their design, and how they made decisions when designing the tour.

Questions are drawn from the video presentation submissions on how students weighed tourist, operator and destination evidence, developed their tour idea, identified what mattered for the community, and translated those judgements into a practical tour design. Students should be prepared to explain their reasoning in their own words, including the strengths and limits of their evidence and how they used or rejected AI-generated material.

This is a secure, identity-verified assessment conducted without notes or electronic devices.

For team submissions, each student is questioned individually to provide evidence of their own understanding and contribution. There is no written submission for this task.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: No late submissions as this is an in-person oral exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

INTRODUCTION

Introduction to the course, staff, program of learning, approach to learning and assessment items. What is Special Interest Tourism?


Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: INTRODUCTION Introduction to the course, staff, program of learning, approach to learning and assessment items. What is Special Interest Tourism?

learning_activities.topic:
SIT BASICS

SIT is perhaps the most rapidly developing type of tourism. What are the characteristics of SIT that make it increasingly popular? What issues arise from this expansion? What theory has developed to explain and predict SIT?
This week we will have a comprehensive introduction to your individual research paper on a SIT type.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
